segments.text: We are going to do the multiplication of these two thousand ohms using a notable product called sum by difference.
We start by changing the position of these two summands, that is, there we are going to apply the commutative property of the sum.
The order of the summands does not alter the result.
So we are going to start writing this factor over here, as we said, changing the position of these two terms.
We start with three tenths of G raised to the exponent A plus one, and then we have plus four thirteenths of H, which is raised to the exponent of minus two.
We protect this with parentheses, and that is multiplied by all this binomial, which is three tenths of G raised to the exponent A plus one,
and that is minus four thirteenths of H raised to the exponent of minus two.
There we have also applied the commutative property of multiplication, because we have changed the position of the factors.
Remember that the order of the factors does not alter the product.
Now we have everything ready to apply the notable product that we mentioned, and that is called sum by difference.
The sum of two quantities multiplied by the difference of them is equal to the first quantity squared minus the second quantity squared.
It is a notable product of great importance that saves us the process of applying the distributive property.
The result is quickly obtained by raising each of the two quantities squared and applying the remaining operation between them.
So let's apply that in this situation.
We start with the first term, which is three tenths of G raised to the exponent of A plus one, and all this raised to the square.
Then we have minus the second term, which is four thirteenths of H raised to the exponent of minus two, and all this raised to the square.
Now we have to develop those powers.
In each case we have the multiplication of two quantities and all that raised to the square.
Then we apply this property of the power.
The product of two quantities raised to an exponent of N is equal to A to the N times B to the N.
That is, the exponent is distributed for each of the factors.
So here we will have three tenths, all this raised to the square, and that by G raised to the exponent of A plus one, and also all this raised to the square.
Then we have minus four thirteenths, which is raised to the square, and that multiplied by the other component H raised to the exponent of minus two, and all this raised to the square.
Now, in these fractions that are raised to the square, another property of the power is applied.
If we have A over B and this raised to the exponent of N, then it is equal to A to the N over B to the N.
That is, the exponent affects both the numerator and the denominator.
Then, for the case of three tenths to the square, that will be three to the square over ten to the square.
And here we have another situation that is called the power of a power.
If we have a power A to the N raised to another exponent of M, the base is preserved and the exponents are multiplied.
Another property of the power.
So here we keep the base, which is G, and the exponents are going to be multiplied.
In that case, it is protected with parentheses A plus one, because it is going to be multiplied by two.
Let's move on to the other term.
Again, the fraction raised to the square, we apply this property.
That is, four to the square over thirteen to the square.
And here we apply again the power of a power.
We keep the base, which is H, and we have the product of the exponents.
B minus two between parentheses multiplied by two.
We now solve each of those situations.
Here we have three to the square, which is nine, over ten to the square, which is one hundred.
And here we have G raised to the exponent, which results in multiplying those two quantities.
There we apply the distributive property.
So we have two times A, which is two A plus two times one, which is two.
Let's move on to the other term.
We have four to the square, which is sixteen.
This over three to the square, which gives us 169.
And here we apply again the distributive property to solve this multiplication.
So we have two times B, which is two B, minus two times two, which is four.
In this way we finish the exercise.
This expression is the answer to this multiplication of binomials.
That we have solved by applying the notable product called sum by difference.
